section.title: The Covenant at Shechem
section.sectionRationale: The book ends where the faith of Israel is put to its sharpest test: a covenant renewal at Shechem. Joshua rehearses the whole story of God's grace, from Abraham to the conquest, all of it God's doing, and then presses the decision: choose this day whom you will serve. The people vow to serve the LORD, a stone is set up as witness, and the book closes with the deaths of Joshua, Joseph's long-carried bones, and Eleazar. It is one movement, the covenant at Shechem, the climactic call to serve the LORD alone in response to His grace.
section.thesis: Because the LORD did everything, taking Abraham, redeeming Israel, giving a land they did not toil for, the only fitting response is to fear and serve Him alone, so Joshua summons the people to a deliberate, witnessed choice, and closes the book with a settled people, a buried patriarch's fulfilled hope, and a covenant sealed by grace.
section.frameNote: Four beats: God's mighty acts rehearsed from Abraham to the conquest, verses 1 to 13; the call to choose whom to serve and the people's vow, verses 14 to 24; the covenant sealed with a stone of witness, verses 25 to 28; and the closing deaths and burials, verses 29 to 33.

units.label: God's mighty acts rehearsed
units.note: I took, I gave, I brought, I sent, all of grace

verses.text: Then Joshua assembled all the tribes of Israel at Shechem. He summoned the elders, leaders, judges, and officers of Israel, and they presented themselves before God.

And Joshua said to all the people, “This is what the LORD, the God of Israel, says: ‘Long ago your fathers, including Terah the father of Abraham and Nahor, lived beyond the Euphrates and worshiped other gods.
But I took your father Abraham from beyond the Euphrates and led him through all the land of Canaan, and I multiplied his descendants. I gave him Isaac,
and to Isaac I gave Jacob and Esau. I gave Esau Mount Seir to possess, but Jacob and his sons went down to Egypt.
Then I sent Moses and Aaron, and I afflicted the Egyptians by what I did there, and afterward I brought you out.
When I brought your fathers out of Egypt and you reached the Red Sea, the Egyptians pursued them with chariots and horsemen as far as the Red Sea.

So your fathers cried out to the LORD, and He put darkness between you and the Egyptians, over whom He brought the sea and engulfed them. Your very eyes saw what I did to the Egyptians. Then you lived in the wilderness for a long time.

Later, I brought you to the land of the Amorites who lived beyond the Jordan. They fought against you, but I delivered them into your hand, that you should possess their land when I destroyed them before you.
Then Balak son of Zippor, the king of Moab, set out to fight against Israel. He sent for Balaam son of Beor to curse you,
but I would not listen to Balaam. So he blessed you again and again, and I delivered you from his hand.

After this, you crossed the Jordan and came to Jericho. The people of Jericho fought against you, as did the Amorites, Perizzites, Canaanites, Hittites, Girgashites, Hivites, and Jebusites, and I delivered them into your hand.
I sent the hornet ahead of you, and it drove out the two Amorite kings before you, but not by your own sword or bow.
So I gave you a land on which you did not toil and cities that you did not build, and now you live in them and eat from vineyards and olive groves that you did not plant.’

units.label: Choose whom you will serve
units.note: as for me and my house, we will serve the LORD

verses.text: Now, therefore, fear the LORD and serve Him in sincerity and truth; cast aside the gods your fathers served beyond the Euphrates and in Egypt, and serve the LORD.

But if it is unpleasing in your sight to serve the LORD, then {{bachar|choose}} for yourselves this day whom you will serve, whether the gods your fathers served beyond the Euphrates, or the gods of the Amorites in whose land you are living. As for me and my house, we will {{abad|serve}} the LORD!”
The people replied, “Far be it from us to forsake the LORD to serve other gods!

For the LORD our God brought us and our fathers out of the land of Egypt, out of the house of slavery, and performed these great signs before our eyes. He also protected us throughout our journey and among all the nations through which we traveled.
And the LORD drove out before us all the nations, including the Amorites who lived in the land. We too will serve the LORD, because He is our God!”
But Joshua said to the people, “You are not able to serve the LORD, for He is a holy God; He is a jealous God; He will not forgive your rebellion or your sins.
If you forsake the LORD and serve foreign gods, He will turn and bring disaster on you and consume you, even after He has been good to you.”
“No!” replied the people. “We will serve the LORD!”
Then Joshua told them, “You are witnesses against yourselves that you have chosen to serve the LORD.” “We are witnesses!” they said.
“Now, therefore,” he said, “get rid of the foreign gods among you and incline your hearts to the LORD, the God of Israel.”
So the people said to Joshua, “We will serve the LORD our God and obey His voice.”

units.label: The covenant sealed with a stone of witness
units.note: this stone will be a witness against us

verses.text: On that day Joshua made a {{berit|covenant}} for the people, and there at Shechem he established for them a statute and ordinance.
Joshua recorded these things in the Book of the Law of God. Then he took a large stone and set it up there under the oak that was near the sanctuary of the LORD.

And Joshua said to all the people, “You see this stone. It will be a witness against us, for it has heard all the words the LORD has spoken to us, and it will be a witness against you if you ever deny your God.”
Then Joshua sent the people away, each to his own inheritance.

units.label: The closing deaths and burials
units.note: Israel served the LORD all the days of Joshua

verses.text: Some time later, Joshua son of Nun, the servant of the LORD, died at the age of 110.
And they buried him in the land of his inheritance, at Timnath-serah in the hill country of Ephraim, north of Mount Gaash.
Israel had served the LORD throughout the days of Joshua and of the elders who outlived him and who had experienced all the works that the LORD had done for Israel.

And the bones of Joseph, which the Israelites had brought up out of Egypt, were buried at Shechem in the plot of land that Jacob had purchased from the sons of Hamor, Shechem’s father, for a hundred pieces of silver. So it became an inheritance for Joseph’s descendants.
Eleazar son of Aaron also died, and they buried him at Gibeah, which had been given to his son Phinehas in the hill country of Ephraim.

bachar.gloss: To choose, select, decide for. Joshua calls Israel to bachar this day whom they will serve. The word presses for a deliberate, conscious decision; there is no drifting into covenant loyalty. Yet the deeper truth of the book is that God first chose Israel, taking Abraham by grace, so their choosing is a response to His prior choice, an answering of grace with allegiance.

abad.gloss: To serve, worship, work for. The verb sounds like a refrain through the chapter: serve the LORD, whom will you serve, we will serve the LORD. To abad the LORD is to give Him exclusive worship and obedient allegiance, the opposite of serving other gods. The whole covenant renewal turns on whom Israel will serve, and Joshua models the answer: as for me and my house, we will serve the LORD.

berit.gloss: Covenant, a binding, sworn relationship. Joshua cuts a berit for the people at Shechem, renewing their bond with the LORD, complete with statutes, a written record, and a stone of witness. The covenant frames Israel's whole life as a sworn relationship with God, one that Israel could not finally keep, pointing ahead to the new covenant secured in Christ.

basic.context: The book climaxes with a covenant renewal at Shechem, the place where Abraham first built an altar in the land. Joshua speaks for God and retells the whole story of Israel, and the striking thing is that almost every verb has God as its subject: I took Abraham, I gave, I brought you out, I sent, I delivered, I gave you a land you did not toil for. It is grace from beginning to end. On that basis, Joshua presses the decision: choose today whom you will serve, and he declares his own resolve, as for me and my house, we will serve the LORD. The people vow to serve the LORD; Joshua soberly warns they are not able to serve a holy God on their own, yet they insist, and he makes them witnesses against themselves. A covenant is sealed and a stone set up as witness. The book ends with the deaths of Joshua and Eleazar and the burial of Joseph's bones, carried up from Egypt at last. The question underneath is: how will God's people respond to a lifetime of grace?
basic.hermeneutics: This is historical narrative recording a covenant ceremony, the theological summit of the book. The rehearsal of God's acts grounds the call to serve Him in grace, not merit. Joshua's warning that they are not able to serve the LORD is a sober note about human inability that points beyond the old covenant to the need for a new heart. We read the famous choose this day within its context of response to God's prior, saving initiative.
basic.originalLanguages: Joshua calls Israel to bachar, to choose, whom they will abad, serve; the verb serve rings through the chapter. On that day he cuts a berit, a covenant, for the people, sealed by a stone of witness.
basic.typology: Joseph's bones, carried from Egypt and buried in the land at last, are faith's fulfillment, a man who died trusting God's promise and is finally brought home. Joshua leading Israel to serve the LORD in the land points to the greater Joshua who brings His people into the true inheritance and enables their service.
basic.christ: Joshua's sober word, you are not able to serve the LORD, exposes a need the old covenant could not meet, and it points to Christ, who serves the Father perfectly, bears our covenant-breaking, and gives a new heart. The whole grace-drenched history Joshua recounts culminates in Christ, God's greatest I took, I gave, I sent, sending His Son to redeem His people.
basic.god: God is the God of sovereign grace, the acting subject of Israel's whole story; everything they have is His gift. He is holy and jealous, worthy of exclusive service. And He is faithful, keeping His promises across generations, so that even Joseph's centuries-old hope is honored. Grace, holiness, and faithfulness meet in Him.
basic.crossReferences: Genesis 50 records Joseph's dying charge that his bones be carried to the land. Deuteronomy 30 sets before Israel life and death, blessing and curse, urging them to choose life. Jeremiah 31 promises the new covenant. And Hebrews 11 lists Joseph's faith about his bones among the heroes of faith.
basic.oneStory: At Shechem, Joshua rehearses the whole history of God's grace and calls Israel to choose to serve the LORD alone, sealing a covenant sealed by grace. The book closes with a settled people, a covenant renewed, and Joseph's long-carried bones buried at last, faith fulfilled. It all points to Christ, the culmination of God's saving acts, who keeps the covenant His people could not and brings them home.

context.literary: Chapter 24 is the grand finale of Joshua, a full covenant-renewal ceremony that echoes the structure of ancient covenants: historical prologue, verses 2 to 13, stipulations and call to allegiance, verses 14 to 24, and the sealing with a written record and a stone witness, verses 25 to 28. The heavy repetition of the verb serve and the first-person divine acts, I took, I gave, I brought, are its most striking features. The closing burials, verses 29 to 33, form an epilogue that ends the era of Joshua and quietly bridges to the book of Judges.
context.historical: Shechem, between Mount Ebal and Mount Gerizim, was rich in covenant memory: Abraham built his first altar there, Genesis 12:6-7, and Israel had earlier renewed the covenant there under Joshua, chapter 8. Reciting a benefactor's deeds before demanding loyalty followed the pattern of ancient treaties. Joseph had made Israel swear to carry his bones to the promised land, Genesis 50:25, a charge kept through the exodus and now fulfilled, closing a hope some four centuries old. The deaths of Joshua and Eleazar mark the passing of the conquest generation, setting the stage for the spiritual decline of Judges.
context.canonical: The chapter distills the theology of Joshua and of the whole Pentateuch: God's saving initiative grounds the call to serve Him. Its choose this day whom you will serve echoes Deuteronomy 30's setting of life and death before Israel and reverberates through Scripture as the summons to wholehearted allegiance. Joshua's warning that they are not able to serve a holy God anticipates the failure that fills Judges and Kings and the promise of a new covenant with a new heart, Jeremiah 31 and Ezekiel 36. Joseph's buried bones fulfill a patriarchal hope and stand in the roll of faith, Hebrews 11:22, testifying that God keeps His promises across the centuries.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To bring the book to its climax in a covenant renewal that grounds Israel's service of the LORD in His sovereign grace, to summon the people to a deliberate, witnessed choice to serve Him alone, and to close the era with the deaths of the conquest generation and the fulfillment of Joseph's hope, all testifying to God's faithfulness.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The specific covenant ceremony belongs to Israel's history. What endures is its theology and its summons: God's grace comes first and grounds all obedience, His people are called to a conscious, whole-hearted choice to serve Him alone, and the sober truth of human inability points to the need for the new heart God gives in Christ. Choose this day remains a fitting call to allegiance for every hearer.

debates.question: Why does Joshua say, you are not able to serve the LORD, right after the people vow to do so?

views.label: A sober exposure of human inability apart from grace
views.case: Joshua confronts a glib, self-confident vow with the holiness and jealousy of God and the people's proven bent to idolatry; he is not forbidding them to serve God but stripping away presumption, showing that serving a holy God is beyond mere human resolve, a truth that drives toward the need for a new heart and points to the new covenant, Jeremiah 31:33.

views.label: A rhetorical test to deepen their commitment
views.case: By pushing back against their easy answer, Joshua forces the people to count the cost and reaffirm their choice more soberly, verses 21 to 24, so the warning functions to make their commitment deliberate and witnessed rather than casual, guarding against the shallow enthusiasm that would soon collapse in Judges.

debates.bearing: Both readings agree Joshua refuses cheap, presumptuous commitment: serving the holy LORD is no light thing, and the people's inability, soon proved in Judges, points beyond the old covenant to the grace and new heart God gives in Christ.

study.originalLanguages: Three words carry the climax of the book. The first is bachar, to choose: choose for yourselves this day whom you will serve, verse 15. The command presses for a deliberate, conscious decision; no one drifts into covenant loyalty. Yet the whole speech has just shown that God chose first, taking Abraham by sheer grace, so Israel's choosing is the answering of grace with allegiance, not the origin of the relationship. The second, sounding like a refrain, is abad, to serve, to worship: serve the LORD, whom will you serve, we will serve the LORD; the verb rings through the chapter fifteen times or more, because the one question is whom Israel will give its exclusive worship and obedience. Joshua models the answer with unforgettable resolve: as for me and my house, we will serve the LORD. The third is berit, covenant: Joshua cuts a berit for the people, complete with statutes, a written record, and a great stone set up as a witness. Choose, serve, covenant: the vocabulary gathers the whole book into a single summons, to answer God's grace by serving Him alone in a binding, witnessed bond, a bond Israel could not finally keep and that points to the new covenant in Christ.

typology.type: Joseph's bones carried from Egypt and buried in the land
typology.fulfillment: Faith's hope fulfilled, a man who died trusting God's promise and is brought home, foreshadowing the resurrection hope in Christ, Hebrews 11:22 and 1 Corinthians 15:20.

typology.type: Joshua leading Israel to serve the LORD in the inheritance
typology.fulfillment: The greater Joshua who brings His people into the true inheritance and enables their service by the Spirit, Hebrews 4:8-9 and Philippians 2:13.

typology.type: A covenant Israel vowed but could not keep
typology.fulfillment: The new covenant in Christ, who keeps it perfectly and writes God's law on the heart, Jeremiah 31:31-34 and Hebrews 8:6-13.

study.christ: The whole covenant renewal is built on grace, and grace points to Christ. Joshua's history is a chain of divine verbs, I took, I gave, I brought out, I sent, I delivered, I gave you a land you did not toil for; salvation is God's doing from first to last, and it reaches its climax when God performs His greatest act of all, He gave His only Son, sending Him to redeem a people who could never redeem themselves, John 3:16. On that grace Joshua bases the call to serve the LORD, and his sober warning, you are not able to serve the LORD, for He is holy, exposes exactly why we need Christ. The people's confident vow would collapse in the idolatry of Judges, proving that human resolve cannot keep covenant with a holy God. But Christ served the Father perfectly, kept the covenant we break, bore the disaster our unfaithfulness deserved, and inaugurated the new covenant in which God writes His law on the heart and works in us both to will and to do His good pleasure, Jeremiah 31:33 and Philippians 2:13. And Joseph's bones, carried for centuries and buried at last in the land, testify that God keeps His promises through death itself, a hope fulfilled in the risen Christ, the firstfruits of all who trust Him. Choose this day whom you will serve finds its truest answer in the One who first chose and served us.
study.god: The God who fills this final chapter is the God of sovereign, initiating grace. From Abraham beyond the Euphrates, worshiping other gods, to Israel eating from vineyards they did not plant, every good thing traces back to God's action: He took, He gave, He brought out, He sent, He delivered. Israel is His workmanship, not His employer. This God is also holy and jealous, and He will not be served alongside idols or with glib, divided hearts; Joshua will not let the people presume upon Him. And He is faithful across the centuries, honoring even Joseph's ancient hope by bringing his bones home to the land. Grace, holiness, and faithfulness stand together in Him. The only fitting response to such a God is the one Joshua models, wholehearted, exclusive service: as for me and my house, we will serve the LORD. And where His people cannot render that service, this same God provides it in His Son and works it in them by His Spirit.

crossReferences.note: Joseph made Israel swear to carry his bones to the promised land, the hope fulfilled in verse 32.
Moses sets life and death before Israel and urges them to choose life, the pattern of Joshua's choose this day.
The promised new covenant with the law written on the heart, meeting the inability Joshua exposed.
By faith Joseph, at the end of his life, gave instructions about his bones, honored in this chapter.
God works in His people to will and to act for His good pleasure, enabling the service the people could not render alone.

study.oneStory: At Shechem, Joshua rehearses the whole sweep of God's grace, from Abraham to the conquest, and on that basis summons Israel to choose to serve the LORD alone, sealing a covenant witnessed by a stone. The book closes with a settled people, the passing of the conquest generation, and Joseph's long-carried bones buried at last, a promise kept across four centuries. All of it, God's initiating grace, the call to serve, the covenant Israel could not keep, and faith's fulfilled hope, points to Christ, the culmination of God's saving acts, who keeps the covenant, gives a new heart, and brings His people home to the inheritance forever.

quiz.q: What is striking about how Joshua retells Israel's history at Shechem?

options.text: Nearly every action is God's: I took, I gave, I brought out, I sent, showing salvation is all of grace.
options.note: Verses 2 to 13 make God the acting subject throughout, grounding the call in grace.

options.text: It focuses on Israel's own achievements and strength.
options.note: The opposite: the emphasis is on God's acts, not Israel's achievements; even the land was one they did not toil for.

options.text: It centers on Joshua's personal leadership.
options.note: Joshua points to God's acts, not his own leadership, as the story's driving force.

options.text: It omits the exodus and the conquest.
options.note: It recounts the exodus, wilderness, and conquest in detail as God's mighty acts.

quiz.q: What decision did Joshua press upon the people?

options.text: To choose that day whom they would serve, and to serve the LORD alone.
options.note: Verses 14 to 15 call for a deliberate choice; Joshua vows, as for me and my house, we will serve the LORD.

options.text: To choose a new leader to succeed him.
options.note: The choice was about whom to serve, God or idols, not about a successor.

options.text: To decide which land each tribe would take.
options.note: The land was already divided; the choice here was whom they would serve.

options.text: To vote on whether to keep the covenant law.
options.note: It was a call to serve the LORD wholeheartedly, not a vote on the law.

quiz.q: Why did Joshua warn, you are not able to serve the LORD?

options.text: To confront glib presumption with God's holiness, exposing that serving Him is beyond mere human resolve.
options.note: Verse 19 sobers their easy vow, pointing to the need for a new heart.

options.text: To tell them God did not want their service at all.
options.note: God did want their service; Joshua was exposing their inability apart from grace, not rejecting them.

options.text: Because he doubted God could help them.
options.note: Joshua doubted their self-confident resolve, not God's power; the issue was human inability.

options.text: To encourage them to serve other gods instead.
options.note: The opposite: he urged them to serve the LORD alone and put away foreign gods.

quiz.q: How does the burial of Joseph's bones close the book meaningfully?

options.text: It fulfills Joseph's centuries-old hope, showing God keeps His promises across generations, even through death.
options.note: Verse 32 fulfills Genesis 50:25, a testimony to God's faithfulness and faith's hope.

options.text: It shows the Israelites had forgotten their history.
options.note: The opposite: they remembered and fulfilled Joseph's charge, honoring the promise.

options.text: It proves Joseph had no real faith in God's promise.
options.note: Joseph's charge about his bones was an act of faith in the promise, honored here, Hebrews 11:22.

options.text: It has nothing to do with God's faithfulness.
options.note: It is a powerful sign of God's faithfulness, fulfilling a promise made four centuries earlier.

quiz.q: How does the covenant at Shechem point to Christ?

options.text: God's initiating grace and the covenant Israel could not keep point to Christ, who keeps it, bears its curse, and gives a new heart.
options.note: The grace-based call and human inability point to the new covenant in Christ, Jeremiah 31:33.

options.text: It teaches that people keep covenant with God by willpower alone.
options.note: Israel's later failure shows willpower is not enough; the answer is Christ and the new heart He gives.

options.text: It shows God's grace ended with the conquest.
options.note: God's grace continues and culminates in Christ, the greatest of His saving acts.

options.text: It has no connection to Christ.
options.note: The grace-drenched history and the unkept covenant point clearly to Christ and the new covenant.

questions: Joshua grounded the call to serve God in all that God had graciously done. How does remembering God's grace to you fuel your own desire to serve Him?
Choose this day whom you will serve is a call to a deliberate decision, not drifting. Is there any area where you are drifting rather than consciously choosing to serve the LORD?
Joshua declared, as for me and my house, we will serve the LORD. What would it look like for you to lead your own household or sphere in serving God?
Joshua soberly said the people were not able to serve a holy God on their own. How does honestly facing your own inability drive you to depend on Christ and the new heart He gives?
Joseph's bones were carried for centuries until God's promise was fulfilled. What long-awaited promise of God are you holding onto, and how does His proven faithfulness strengthen your hope?

translationNotes.note: Beyond the Euphrates renders a phrase the BSB footnote gives as beyond the River; it recurs in verses 3, 14, and 15, recalling that Abraham's family had worshiped other gods before God called him.
The hornet that drove out the enemy renders an image, echoing Exodus 23:28, of God's own power, not by your own sword or bow, winning the land.
The oak near the sanctuary is rendered terebinth in the BSB footnote; Shechem was long associated with covenant, from Abraham's altar, Genesis 12:6-7.
A hundred pieces of silver renders a hundred kesitahs, an ancient unit whose exact value is no longer known, as the BSB footnote notes; the burial fulfills Joseph's charge in Genesis 50:25.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for Joshua 24:1-33. Prose covenant-renewal narrative with speeches; a line-break marker in verse 22 was ignored and the segments joined with a single space. No em or en dashes present in the source or introduced; none anywhere in the file. Sermon left as none; no sermon URL was verified this session.
